video_title: Tom Mueller: SpaceX's #1 Employee Built the Merlin Engine. Now He's Building Impulse Space
gist: Tom Mueller, SpaceX founding propulsion lead and Merlin-engine designer, tours his startup Impulse Space, which is building in-space transportation: the Mirror orbital maneuvering vehicle, the Helios kick-stage, and a high-performance methalox engine (Denib). He frames launch as 'mostly solved' and in-space mobility plus cislunar/lunar logistics as the next investable frontier, and argues compute and resource extraction will inevitably move off Earth.

themes.theme: In-space transportation as the next frontier post-launch

insights.claim: Tom Mueller's thesis is that Earth-to-orbit launch is 'mostly solved' by SpaceX, so the next big opportunity is moving payload and cargo around once it is already in space, which is the entire reason he founded Impulse Space.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller (founder/CEO/CTO, Impulse Space)
insights.so_what: Positions in-space mobility / orbital transfer as the next distinct space-economy layer to back, separate from launch incumbents; Impulse is a pure-play on it.
insights.quote: the next big opportunity is to move all that payload, all that cargo around in space

insights.claim: Impulse Space's product stack is a two-part architecture: Mirror, a highly propulsive orbital maneuvering vehicle that can stay on orbit up to five years, and Helios, a kick-stage that adds an effective third stage to a two-stage rocket to deliver payloads to high-energy orbits.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Clarifies Impulse's dual product lines for anyone tracking the orbital-transfer-vehicle (OTV) competitive set vs. rivals like Momentus, Launcher/Vast, or Atomos.

insights.claim: Helios can take a 4-ton satellite from low Earth orbit to geostationary orbit, deliver up to 5 tons of payload to the Moon on a single Falcon 9, and improve payload to Mars by up to a factor of five.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Quantifies the performance leverage of a dedicated kick-stage riding on cheap Falcon 9 capacity, the economic basis for the OTV business case.
insights.quote: It can take, you know, all five tons of payload to the moon on a Falcon 9. It can improve your payload to Mars by up to a factor of five.

insights.claim: Impulse already has three Mirror spacecraft on orbit, performed an autonomous rendezvous bringing two of them within 1,200 meters of each other, and had to execute a collision-avoidance maneuver within the first three to four months of operations.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Demonstrated on-orbit rendezvous and maneuvering is a hard technical milestone that de-risks Impulse vs. paper-stage competitors and signals readiness for servicing/inspection missions.
insights.quote: We did a rendezvous last year between two of them. Got within 1,200 meters of each other, which is really damn close in space.

insights.claim: Impulse Space is also bidding on lunar lander work, including human landers on the Moon, which Mueller envisions powered by a scaled-up multi-engine version of its Denib engine.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Signals Impulse intends to compete for NASA/commercial lunar-lander contracts, expanding its addressable market beyond OTVs into cislunar logistics.

themes.theme: Propulsion edge: Denib engine and performance records

insights.claim: Impulse's Denib is an oxygen-rich staged-combustion methalox engine producing ~15,000 lbf of thrust, which Mueller claims will be the highest-performing hydrocarbon engine ever flown thanks to very high mixing efficiency and a large high-area-ratio carbon nozzle.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: A record-class in-house engine is the core IP moat for Impulse and the basis for its claim to be the 'prime mover' across its product line and future landers.
insights.quote: this engine will probably be the highest performing hydrocarbon engine ever flown because it has a very high area ratio

insights.claim: Mueller built the Merlin engine at SpaceX, which he states is the most reliable rocket engine ever developed and has the highest thrust-to-weight of any rocket engine ever developed, and he led propulsion for Falcon, Dragon, and the origins of Starship.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Founder pedigree is a key diligence signal: the person who built SpaceX's flagship engine is the technical core of Impulse, lowering execution risk on the engine roadmap.
insights.quote: the Merlin engine which is currently flying on Falcon 9, the most reliable rocket engine ever developed and also the highest thrust to weight of any rocket engine ever developed

insights.claim: The Denib engine targets an ISP of roughly 385-400 seconds versus ~290 seconds for Impulse's small Saif thruster, and its carbon-ceramic nozzle skirt is expected to run over 3,000 degrees F, which Mueller claims would be the hottest nozzle ever flown in space.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Specific-impulse and thermal records are the quantitative performance edge that justifies premium pricing and competitiveness for high-energy and lunar missions.
insights.quote: This this engine has about a 290 second ISP. Whereas the DNA engine will have almost 400 second ISP.

insights.claim: Oxygen-rich staged combustion is, in Mueller's words, a 'very difficult development,' and Impulse is only now finishing turbopump development and beginning engine testing, signaling Denib is still pre-flight maturity.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Flags the key technical risk and timeline gap on Impulse's flagship engine for anyone underwriting the company's near-term lunar/GEO ambitions.
insights.quote: oxid stage combustion is a difficult very difficult development

insights.claim: Mueller states it takes three iterations to reach a 'really tight' rocket-engine product, citing that both Merlin and Raptor required three versions to mature, a reusable framework for judging where an engine program sits on its development curve.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: A heuristic investors can apply to any new-space engine program to gauge maturity and remaining iteration risk before flight readiness.
insights.quote: it takes really three three iterations to get to a really really tight product

themes.theme: Vertical integration and advanced manufacturing as moat

insights.claim: Impulse Space is extremely vertically integrated, running its own machine shop, assembly, propulsion, and test areas on-site to build, assemble, test, and iterate rapidly, a model Mueller explicitly carried over from SpaceX ('vertically integrated, hire the best, move fast').
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Vertical integration plus fast iteration is the operational playbook that produced SpaceX's cost advantage; its replication is a key bull signal for Impulse's margins and speed.
insights.quote: Vertically integrated, hire the best and move fast. That that's what we learned at SpaceX.

insights.claim: Mueller calls metal 3D printing 'almost like a cheat code' for rocket engines because complex injector and cooling passages can be drawn and printed directly, dramatically easing what is otherwise difficult machining, welding, and brazing.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Reinforces additive manufacturing as a structural cost/speed advantage in new-space propulsion, relevant to suppliers like Velo3D and EOS and to the broader make-vs-buy thesis.
insights.quote: For rocket engines, it's almost like a cheat code. It's so easy to make a high performance rocket engine with 3D printing

insights.claim: Impulse bought its metal 3D printers within the first year of founding and runs three in-house, including Velo3D machines printing a proprietary burn-resistant alloy called Novaloy for hot-oxygen parts like the turbopump and main injector.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Names Velo3D as a printer supplier to Impulse and flags a proprietary alloy as IP, useful for mapping the additive-manufacturing supply chain in new space.

insights.claim: Impulse brought composite overwrapped pressure vessel (COPV) tank production in-house early because tanks are a major portion of spacecraft mass and a properly designed COPV weighs about half of a state-of-the-art all-metallic titanium tank.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Mass reduction directly improves payload economics; controlling COPV production internally is both a performance and a supply-chain-resilience advantage.
insights.quote: a properly designed COPV will be about half the mass of a state-of-the-art all metallic tank like a titanium spacecraft sphere

insights.claim: COPVs are hard to source from industry, with quoted lead times of one to two years and prices of hundreds of thousands of dollars apiece, which is why Impulse insourced their production to control quality, quantity, and cost.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Quantifies a real space-supply-chain bottleneck (long lead, high cost on pressure vessels) that creates both insourcing pressure and an opportunity for merchant suppliers.
insights.quote: if you wanted to buy one of these, it might take, you know, after you order it might take a year or two to get them and be hundreds of thousands of dollars a piece

themes.theme: Propulsion roadmap: electric, solar-electric, and nuclear

insights.claim: Impulse uses Krypton-fueled Hall-effect electric thrusters (the same type used by Starlink) for north-south station-keeping in geostationary orbit, preserving chemical propellant for fast customer maneuvers, blending electric and chemical propulsion in one OTV.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: A hybrid electric+chemical architecture differentiates Impulse from all-electric GEO satellites; krypton/hall-thruster supply chain is a watch-item.
insights.quote: That's a hall thruster just like Starlink has. It runs on Krypton.

insights.claim: Mueller predicts solar-electric propulsion will remain the dominant form for cislunar and Earth-Mars transport on cost and performance, especially as space solar panels get very light, while nuclear-electric propulsion becomes important only for the outer planets.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Sets a hierarchy for where to expect propulsion R&D dollars and contracts; tempers near-term nuclear-propulsion hype for Earth-Mars routes.

insights.claim: Mueller is a strong proponent of nuclear-electric propulsion for the outer planets but expects big nuclear stages are likely a few decades away absent more funding, noting NASA's SPACE Reactor 1 (SR1) Mars mission is starting the timeline now.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Frames nuclear propulsion as a long-dated, funding-dependent theme rather than a near-term investable wave; SR1 is the early demonstrator to track.
insights.quote: I think it's pretty far in the future... maybe a few decades away before we're really doing big nuclear stages

insights.claim: Mueller argues a nuclear-electric stage would transform deep-space science economics by letting probes slow down and enter orbit around distant bodies like Pluto rather than flying past, and by powering instruments and radios for decades, unlike fading RTG-powered Voyager.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Articulates the long-term value case for nuclear propulsion that could justify future NASA/government funding flows into the sector.

themes.theme: Compute and resources moving off Earth

insights.claim: Mueller's hot take is that compute must move to space because it only needs power as an input and produces data as an output, making it the ideal megastructure to build off-planet, with data beamed down via terawatt laser links.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: A founder bull case for orbital data centers; if it materializes it expands demand for in-space transport, power, and laser-comms infrastructure that OTVs like Impulse serve.
insights.quote: compute needs to move to space. It's it makes the most sense of anything to move to space to build mega structure because all you need as an input is power and all you have as an output is data.

insights.claim: Mueller cites that compute power demand is growing more than 15% per year and that some project it could equal all of Earth's current base power use within 20-30 years, framing terrestrial power scarcity as the forcing function pushing compute to orbit.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Quantifies the power-constraint thesis behind both terrestrial-power scarcity trades and the space-data-center narrative.
insights.quote: compute power for compute is growing at greater than 15% per year. Some people say that it'll be equal to all the earth's base power right now in about 20 or 30 years.

insights.claim: Mueller says the real bottleneck on data centers is how fast power can be procured, citing up to seven years to get power for a new data center today, which is the gap orbital data centers could close.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Frames grid-interconnection delay (up to 7 years) as the binding constraint on AI buildout, a key signal for power, grid, and alternative-siting investments.
insights.quote: it can take, you know, up to seven years right now to get power for a new data center

insights.claim: Mueller predicts copper scarcity (driven heavily by data-center demand) will force space resource extraction from the Moon and asteroids, which he says hold a near-infinite, thousands-of-years supply, and could also drive a shift toward photonic chips.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Links AI/data-center copper demand to both a commodity-scarcity thesis and long-dated space-mining and photonics optionality.
insights.quote: people are predicting we're going to run out of copper here in the next few years. Just data centers require so much copper. We need to start getting copper from the moon, from the asteroids.

themes.theme: Lunar priority, SpaceX read-throughs, and ecosystem

insights.claim: Mueller believes the Moon is more important than Mars in the near term, aligning with Jared Isaacman's stated NASA goal of building and staying at a base on the Moon, which shapes where he is steering Impulse's lander ambitions.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: A Moon-first stance from a top space technologist signals where near-term commercial and NASA contract dollars (and Impulse's strategy) are likely to concentrate.
insights.quote: I think the moon is more important than Mars in the near term.

insights.claim: Mueller recounts that SpaceX's internal Starlink spreadsheets showed compelling ROI from the start, and insiders pushed to build and launch satellites as fast as possible while public naysayers doubted it, foreshadowing the business that became SpaceX's value driver.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Insider confirmation that Starlink's economics were obvious internally early reinforces the pattern that vertically integrated mass-production constellations can hockey-stick valuations.
insights.quote: This is going to, you know, this is going to hockey stick this the stock which it exactly did.

insights.claim: Impulse Space supplied the complete propulsion system, including thrusters, for Vast's space station, indicating Impulse is already a propulsion vendor to commercial space-station developers.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Confirms a B2B revenue channel for Impulse beyond its own OTVs: selling propulsion subsystems to the emerging commercial-station market (Vast, and by extension Axiom/Orbital Reef peers).
insights.quote: We provided the whole propulsion system for vast including the thrusters.

insights.claim: Space-situational-awareness warnings to operators come from both the U.S. Air Force and commercial providers such as LeoLabs, which Impulse relied on for its early collision-avoidance maneuver.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Names LeoLabs as a commercial SSA provider in operational use, a data point for the growing space-traffic-management and tracking market.

insights.claim: Mueller credits Elon Musk's edge to urgency and a talent obsession, stating the key to success is a great team and that recruiting top talent is a core practice he replicates at Impulse.
insights.speaker: guest, Tom Mueller
insights.so_what: Reinforces that talent density and urgency are the soft moats behind new-space outperformance, a qualitative diligence lens for the sector.
insights.quote: the key to success is having a great team
